segments.title: Arriving in Kobe by Shinkansen
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke’s first Shinkansen journey frames an arrival in Kobe through the speed, efficiency, and service of Japanese rail travel.
segments.body: This brief travel note opens with Dr. Clarke’s first trip on the Shinkansen. He describes the ride as efficient and the staff as welcoming, then places himself in Kobe. The moment is a useful on-the-ground introduction to a journey through Japan rather than a technical account of the railway. His reaction also sets up the video’s wider emphasis on encountering a place whose everyday rhythms feel markedly different from his expectations.

evidence.note: He recounts his first Shinkansen journey and arrival in Kobe.

segments.title: Experiencing Japan as a 24-hour country
segments.summary: Late-night food in Tokyo and Yokosuka leads to a traveller’s reflection on Japan’s unusually continuous urban life.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the usual idea of a 24-hour city with his impression of Japan as a 24-hour country. A late-night yakitori stop in Tokyo provides his concrete example: food was available at 2 a.m. on a back street, and he found the same broad availability in Yokosuka. He is careful to present this as an early travel impression, wondering whether it will hold once the trip reaches more rural areas. The note is useful for viewers interested in how daily infrastructure, hospitality, and street life shaped the experience of visiting several Japanese cities.

evidence.note: He reflects on late-night food in Tokyo and Yokosuka and calls Japan a 24-hour country.
